name: Kuwait
summary: NON-COMBATANT UNDER SUSTAINED ATTACK: Iranian drone attack struck fuel tanks at Kuwait International Airport on April 1, setting them ablaze in 'massive blaze' — no casualties, airport already closed to commercial operations since late Feb/early March. Continues pattern: desalination plant (March 30, Indian worker killed), VLCC Al-Salmi (March 31, 2M bbl). Kuwait relies on desalination for ~90% of drinking water; airport is critical evacuation route. Three Iranian attacks on Kuwaiti infrastructure in 72 hours.

signals.content: Iranian missiles and drones targeted Ali Al Salem Air Base and US Embassy in Kuwait City. 4 soldiers and 4 civilians killed.
Drones struck two Kuwaiti refineries, contributing to oil price spike above $112/bbl.

Air raid sirens activated across Kuwait early Sunday March 29. Kuwait Defense Ministry described 'hostile missile and drone attacks.' Simultaneous sirens across Kuwait, Bahrain, and Israel — broadest regional bombardment pattern of the conflict. Iran hitting three countries simultaneously, consistent with CSIS assessment that Iran has abandoned calibrated retaliation for 'unbridled escalation.'

Iranian drone/missile attack struck a power and water desalination plant — 1 Indian worker killed, significant material damage to service building. Kuwait Ministry of Electricity and Water described it as 'sinful Iranian aggression.' Kuwait National Guard separately downed 5 drones. Kuwait relies on seawater desalination for approximately 90% of its drinking water supply, making this infrastructure existentially critical. This represents a NEW TARGETING EXPANSION by Iran: from military→energy→industrial→civilian water/power infrastructure. Iran's targeting doctrine now encompasses any Gulf infrastructure regardless of civilian purpose. (Sources: Al Jazeera, The National Abu Dhabi, Anadolu Agency, India TV, Outlook India, The Week India, CNBC)

Kuwaiti tanker Al-Salmi attacked by Iranian drone at Dubai Port (March 31). Fire extinguished, crew safe. Separate from earlier Kuwait desalination strike (March 30) which killed an Indian worker. Kuwait relies on desalination for ~90% of drinking water. Two Iranian attacks on Kuwaiti interests in 48 hours — tanker and water infrastructure. Kuwait has not issued formal response. CONFIRMED.

Iranian drone struck Kuwait-flagged VLCC Al-Salmi at Dubai's port anchorage zone on March 31 — a fully laden vessel carrying approximately 2 million barrels of Saudi and Kuwaiti oil bound for China. This is the most significant single-vessel attack of the war and the first targeting neutral shipping in a Gulf ally's waters (Dubai anchorage, not Hormuz itself). All 24 crew confirmed safe, no oil spill. Concurrent attacks hit Greek container ship off Ras Tanura and Thai cargo near Hormuz (3 crew missing). Kuwait's desalination plant strike (March 30, Indian worker killed) + this VLCC attack = Kuwait increasingly in the crossfire despite not being a combatant. Kuwait National Guard downed 5 drones. Iran blamed Israel for the desalination strike (no evidence). (Sources: Bloomberg, Gulf News, Al Jazeera, Maritime Executive, The National)

Iranian drone attack struck fuel tanks at Kuwait International Airport on April 1, setting them ablaze in a 'massive blaze.' No casualties reported. Airport had already been closed to commercial operations since late February/early March. Continues the accelerating pattern of Iranian strikes on Gulf civilian infrastructure — Kuwait water desalination plant (March 30, killing one Indian worker), VLCC Al-Salmi tanker (March 31), now airport fuel storage. Kuwait relies on desalination for ~90% of drinking water; the airport is a critical evacuation route. Three Iranian attacks on Kuwaiti infrastructure in 72 hours despite Kuwait not being a combatant. CONFIRMED. (Sources: AGBI, Al Jazeera)

edges.context: Three Iranian attacks on Kuwaiti infrastructure in 72 hours: airport fuel tanks (April 1), tanker Al-Salmi at Dubai Port (March 31), desalination plant (March 30, Indian worker killed). Kuwait not a combatant but absorbing sustained damage. Airport closed, water supply at risk.
Kuwaiti refineries struck, airport fuel tanks ablaze. Adding to supply disruption and logistics collapse.
